body:
### Why this matters

Amazon EKS Kubernetes 1.30 is in extended support and reaches end of extended support on July 23, 2026. Older Kubernetes versions receive fewer fixes, can move into extended support, and may eventually be automatically upgraded by the provider.

### What this control checks

This control finds clusters whose Kubernetes version starts with `1.30`. Upgrade the control plane, node pools, managed add-ons, and workload dependencies before the lifecycle date.

### Operational note

Treat Kubernetes upgrades as platform releases. Check deprecated APIs, admission policies, add-on compatibility, node images, PodDisruptionBudgets, and workload readiness before changing production clusters. The lifecycle date for this check is `2026-07-23`.
name: Ensure Amazon EKS clusters are not running Kubernetes 1.30
description: EKS clusters running Kubernetes 1.30 lifecycle version
timedControlSummary: Amazon EKS clusters on Kubernetes 1.30 should be upgraded before the provider lifecycle date.
timedControlDisruptionRisk: Late EKS upgrades can trigger extended support charges, automatic control plane upgrades, and unplanned node/add-on compatibility work.
remediationDescription:
## AWS remediation

> **Service-wide platform upgrade (recommended):** upgrade clusters by environment or application group instead of fixing one cluster at a time. This lets platform teams validate Kubernetes API removals, EKS add-ons, node AMIs, and workload policies once and reuse the rollout plan.

### AWS Console

1. Open the Amazon EKS console and select the affected cluster.
2. Review the cluster version, upgrade insights, installed add-ons, node groups, and Fargate profiles.
3. Resolve upgrade insight findings, especially removed Kubernetes APIs.
4. Choose **Update now** for the control plane and select a supported Kubernetes version such as `1.34`.
5. After the control plane upgrade completes, update EKS add-ons, managed node groups, self-managed nodes, and restart Fargate workloads.

### AWS CLI

Inspect the current cluster version and upgrade policy:

```bash
aws eks describe-cluster \
  --name <cluster-name> \
  --region <region> \
  --query 'cluster.{name:name,version:version,status:status,upgradePolicy:upgradePolicy}'
```

Review available versions and support dates:

```bash
aws eks describe-cluster-versions \
  --region <region> \
  --query 'clusterVersions[].{version:clusterVersion,status:status,endOfStandardSupport:endOfStandardSupportDate,endOfExtendedSupport:endOfExtendedSupportDate}'
```

Upgrade the control plane:

```bash
aws eks update-cluster-version \
  --name <cluster-name> \
  --region <region> \
  --kubernetes-version 1.34
```

Update managed node groups after the control plane upgrade:

```bash
aws eks update-nodegroup-version \
  --cluster-name <cluster-name> \
  --nodegroup-name <node-group-name> \
  --region <region> \
  --kubernetes-version 1.34
```

### References

- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/kubernetes-versions.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/update-cluster.html

timedControlRules.summary: Amazon EKS clusters on Kubernetes 1.30 should be upgraded before the provider lifecycle date.
timedControlRules.disruptionRisk: Late EKS upgrades can trigger extended support charges, automatic control plane upgrades, and unplanned node/add-on compatibility work.
timedControlRules.recommendedTarget: Upgrade the EKS control plane, node groups, Fargate workloads, and add-ons to Kubernetes 1.34 or another version in standard support.
